decision.recommended_next_action: Close this project as a mixed positive result: keep static hot/cold residency and selective persistent mirrors as the useful mechanism, and do not continue same-project reusable eviction/reload without a materially different graph-partitioned async backend design.
decision.stop_reason: True reusable mirror-buffer eviction/reuse was implemented and measured, but it copied 84-168 GiB per short decode and underperformed static hot/cold residency by about 42-43%.

recommended_next_action: Close this project as a mixed positive result: keep static hot/cold residency and selective persistent mirrors as the useful mechanism, and do not continue same-project reusable eviction/reload without a materially different graph-partitioned async backend design.
run_notes_tail: - True reusable mirror-buffer aliasing is technically possible in this bounded llama.cpp graph-lifecycle experiment: every run returned 0, and the pager reported `status=ready`, `tensors=480`, `swapped=480`, `skipped_layers=0`.
- The reusable arena only needs about 312 MiB for the largest selected cold layer, so 512 MiB, 1 GiB, and 2 GiB arenas all exercised the same reuse behavior.

- The performance result is negative for same-mechanism dynamic eviction/reuse: reusable pager throughput was only 3.28-3.30 tok/s for 8-token decode and 3.31 tok/s for 16-token decode, versus 5.65 and 5.81 tok/s static hot/cold baselines.

- The reason is explicit in trace telemetry: per-token layer eviction/reload copied 84.0 GiB over 8 generated tokens and 168.0 GiB over 16 generated tokens, spending about 1.56 s and 3.10 s respectively inside pager copy work. Increasing arena budget did not help because the bottleneck is repeated cold-layer reload volume, not arena capacity.

- Compared with the earlier persistent fixed-budget pager sweep, persistent consumed-tensor mirrors produced modest positive signals (+1% to +7% in bounded runs), while true reusable eviction/reuse lost about 42-43% versus static. This suggests the useful mechanism is selective persistent migration/residency, not naive decode-step eviction/reuse.
- The reusable pager sweep completed all controls and six reusable pager cases with return code 0.
- `pgrep -af 'llama-bench|llama-server|llama-cli'` showed no remaining project helper inference process after the run.

Finalize this project with a mixed/positive research result. Hot/cold tensor placement under an 8 GiB hot CUDA budget is supported, and consumed persistent cold-tensor mirrors can slightly improve it. The true reusable mirror-buffer eviction/reuse variant requested for the final check is practical but not competitive in this llama.cpp graph lifecycle because it reloads the cold side every decode step and loses roughly 42-43% versus static residency.

- True reusable mirror-buffer aliasing is technically possible in this bounded llama.cpp graph-lifecycle experiment: every run returned 0, and the pager reported `status=ready`, `tensors=480`, `swapped=480`, `skipped_layers=0`.
- The reusable arena only needs about 312 MiB for the largest selected cold layer, so 512 MiB, 1 GiB, and 2 GiB arenas all exercised the same reuse behavior.

- The performance result is negative for same-mechanism dynamic eviction/reuse: reusable pager throughput was only 3.28-3.30 tok/s for 8-token decode and 3.31 tok/s for 16-token decode, versus 5.65 and 5.81 tok/s static hot/cold baselines.

- The reason is explicit in trace telemetry: per-token layer eviction/reload copied 84.0 GiB over 8 generated tokens and 168.0 GiB over 16 generated tokens, spending about 1.56 s and 3.10 s respectively inside pager copy work. Increasing arena budget did not help because the bottleneck is repeated cold-layer reload volume, not arena capacity.

- Compared with the earlier persistent fixed-budget pager sweep, persistent consumed-tensor mirrors produced modest positive signals (+1% to +7% in bounded runs), while true reusable eviction/reuse lost about 42-43% versus static. This suggests the useful mechanism is selective persistent migration/residency, not naive decode-step eviction/reuse.
- The reusable pager sweep completed all controls and six reusable pager cases with return code 0.
- `pgrep -af 'llama-bench|llama-server|llama-cli'` showed no remaining project helper inference process after the run.

Finalize this project with a mixed/positive research result. Hot/cold tensor placement under an 8 GiB hot CUDA budget is supported, and consumed persistent cold-tensor mirrors can slightly improve it. The true reusable mirror-buffer eviction/reuse variant requested for the final check is practical but not competitive in this llama.cpp graph lifecycle because it reloads the cold side every decode step and loses roughly 42-43% versus static residency.
